label: AI

hero.title:
What is AI,
really?
hero.subtitle: (and friends.)
hero.lead: A scribbled-in field guide to AI, agents, chatbots, skills, cron, and agentic UI — explained the way a friend would, not the way a textbook would.
hero.note: ← drag the thumb tabs on the right →

welcomeStickies.html:
✎ How to read this:

scroll. scribble in the reflection boxes (they save). flip the cards. open the "under the hood" if you're curious. skip anything boring.

✎ A promise:

no equations. no ten-syllable words. no "synergy." if a sentence here feels like a textbook, please send it back — i'll rewrite it.

✎ Your job:

argue with the text. write rude things in the margins. that's literally how this works.

tocTitle: The table of contents (also a sticky)

levels.label: BASIC
levels.intro: — the parts you'll meet first. start here, even if you've heard the words before.

sticky.html: Imagine a parrot that has read every book ever written. It can't fly, it can't make coffee — but ask it anything, and it'll squawk back something that sounds weirdly smart.

blocks.html:
That's most of what we call "AI" today. Pattern-matching at a planetary scale. It's not thinking the way you think. It's predicting what word, pixel, or action probably comes next, based on a mountain of examples.

Important: the parrot is sometimes confidently wrong. We have a word for that — two words actually — "hallucination" if you're in research, "vibes" if you're on Twitter.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: parrot in a library, reading every book at once

blocks.title: Under the hood — why does it work at all?
blocks.html:
A modern AI model is a giant function. You feed in text (or images, or audio), it produces numbers, and those numbers vote on the next token. Train it on enough examples and the votes start to look like understanding. Spoiler: it isn't, but the demo is impressive.

The model has billions of parameters — little dials that got nudged during training. Nobody tuned them by hand. That's the magic and the problem.

bad.label: COMMON MISCONCEPTION
bad.html: "AI is a robot brain that thinks and reasons like a human, just faster."

good.label: MORE ACCURATE
good.html: "AI is statistics with a really good vocabulary. It predicts likely outputs from patterns in past data."

blocks.prompt: In your own words: if AI is 'pattern matching,' where would that fail?
blocks.hint: e.g. brand new situations, math, anything requiring lived experience…

lessons.title: A chatbot is a walkie-talkie

sticky.html: Press the button. Say a thing. Let go. Wait. A voice replies. Press the button. Say another thing. Repeat until you're bored.

blocks.html:
That's a chatbot. One turn in, one turn out. You talk, it talks. It doesn't go off and do anything in the world between turns — it just waits with the receiver pressed to its ear.

If the chatbot tries to order pizza for you, congratulations — that's not a chatbot anymore. That's the next lesson.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: two walkie-talkies on a desk, one labeled YOU, one labeled BOT

blocks.label: HOW A CHAT TURN ACTUALLY WORKS
blocks.caption: notice: nothing happens between turns. no memory unless you give it some.

annotations.text: ↑ no buttons, no tools, just words

blocks.html:
Write me a haiku about Mondays.
Sure! Alarm clock screams
Coffee's not strong enough yet —
Mug judges my soul.

blocks.title: Under the hood — context, tokens, and 'memory'
blocks.html:
A chatbot has no real memory. Every turn, the app re-sends the whole conversation back to the model. That's why long chats feel slower — it's literally re-reading everything.

Words get chopped into "tokens" first (roughly: chunks of ~4 characters). The model has a maximum number of tokens it can hold in working memory at once. Exceed it, and the earliest stuff falls off the back of the truck.

items.question: If a chatbot forgets your name after 50 messages, what most likely happened?
items.answer: The earliest messages — including the one where you introduced yourself — got pushed out of the model's context window. The app didn't 'remember' to keep that part.

items.question: Why does the same chatbot give different answers to the same question?
items.answer: The model samples from a probability distribution. There's a 'temperature' setting that controls randomness. Set it to 0 and answers become much more repeatable — and often more boring.

blocks.prompt: Think of a chatbot you've used. What's one thing it couldn't do, that you wished it could?

levels.label: INTERMEDIATE
levels.intro: — now we wire things together. agents, skills, schedules.

lessons.title: An agent is a chef in a kitchen

sticky.html: You say "make me dinner." The chef checks the fridge, decides on pasta, boils water, chops garlic, plates it, brings it out. You didn't say any of those steps. The chef figured them out.

blocks.html:
That's an agent. A chatbot talks. An agent decides what to do, then does it. It picks tools, takes actions, checks the result, and tries again if it failed.

The polite version: "autonomous task execution." The honest version: "a model in a while-loop that occasionally surprises you."
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: chef hat above a pot, with tools (knife, timer, spoon) flying in

bad.label: CHATBOT
bad.html:
"How do I deploy this app?"

→ Returns a 12-step list. You do the steps.

good.label: AGENT
good.html:
"Deploy this app."

→ Reads your repo, picks a host, runs the CLI, fixes the error it hit, tells you the URL.

blocks.title: Under the hood — tools, planning, and the loop
blocks.html:
An agent is usually built around a model that's been told: "Here is a list of tools you can call. To use one, output a structured request. I'll run it and give you the result."

The loop is roughly: model → tool call → result → model → tool call → … → final answer. Each step, the model sees everything that came before. Get the prompt right, and it acts smart. Get it wrong, and it goes shopping for gardening tools when you asked for dinner.

blocks.prompt: Name a task you'd trust to an agent, and one you absolutely wouldn't. What's the difference?

lessons.title: Skills are the agent's tool belt

sticky.html: A plumber doesn't carry every tool ever made. They carry a wrench, a snake, some tape, a flashlight. The right belt for the job.

blocks.html: Skills are the same idea, for agents. A skill is a named ability + instructions for when to use it. "Send an email." "Search the codebase." "Save a PDF." The agent reaches into the belt, grabs the right one, and uses it.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a leather tool belt with labeled pouches — 'send email', 'read pdf', 'book flight'

blocks.label: ANATOMY OF A SKILL
blocks.html:
NAME →save_as_pdf
WHEN →"use this when the user wants a printable document"
HOW →a script, an API call, or instructions to follow step by step

annotations.text: ↗ the skill list, like apps on a phone
↖ each one has a 'when to use it' hint

blocks.html:
send_email
drafts + sends mail
search_web
looks up fresh info
read_pdf
extracts text from PDFs
book_flight
queries travel APIs
make_slide
builds a presentation
+ add skill

blocks.title: Under the hood — the 'when' matters more than the 'how'
blocks.html:
Most people focus on writing the code for a skill. The harder part is the description — the tiny hint that tells the agent when to reach for it.

Bad hint → the agent ignores the skill, or uses it on every single turn like a kid with a new hammer. Good hint → it picks the right tool for the right job. Skill-writing is half engineering, half tour-guide essay.

good.label: GOOD SKILL HINT
good.html: "Use when the user explicitly asks to send, reply to, or schedule an email. Do NOT use for drafting where they only want to read it back."

blocks.prompt: Pick a job you do often. What 3 skills would your personal agent need?

lessons.title: Cron is an alarm clock for software

sticky.html: "Every weekday at 7am, brew the coffee." You don't tell the machine each morning. You set it up once and it goes off forever.

blocks.html:
That's cron. A scheduler that wakes up at specific times and runs a task. It's been around since the 1970s. It's not glamorous. Half the internet runs on it anyway.

If "Why isn't my report there?" leads to "the cron didn't fire", you have officially become an adult in software.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: classic alarm clock with bells, ringing — labeled '0 7 * * 1-5'

blocks.label: ANATOMY OF A CRON EXPRESSION
blocks.html:
0  7  *  *  1-5
↑
minute
↑
hour
↑
day of month
↑
month
↑
day of week

translation: "at minute 0 of hour 7, every day, every month, Monday through Friday."

items.question: What does */15 * * * * mean?
items.answer: Every 15 minutes, every hour, every day. The */N syntax says 'fire every N units of this field.'

items.question: What does 0 0 1 * * mean?
items.answer: At midnight on the first of every month. Useful for monthly reports, billing rollups, and resetting that 'days since incident' counter.

blocks.title: Under the hood — why agents love cron
blocks.html:
A chatbot only acts when you message it. An agent only acts when you give it a task. Cron flips that around: time itself becomes the trigger. Every morning at 7, your agent wakes up, checks your calendar, drafts your daily standup, and emails it before you've poured the coffee.

Cron + agent + skills = software that does things while you sleep. Magical and slightly unsettling. As all good tools should be.

blocks.prompt: What's one recurring chore in your week that you wish a cron-triggered agent would handle?

levels.label: ADVANCED
levels.intro: — what happens when the interface itself starts doing the work.

lessons.title: Agentic UI: the dashboard drives itself

sticky.html:
Old apps: you press the buttons.
New apps: you say what you want; the app presses its own buttons and shows you what changed.

blocks.html: That's agentic UI. An interface where the user expresses intent, and the system takes actions on their behalf — visibly. The screen stops being a control panel and becomes a windshield.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: a car dashboard, but the steering wheel is doing its own thing while the driver watches

bad.label: TRADITIONAL UI
bad.html:
"Click Reports → Filter → Last 30 days → Export → CSV → Download."

5 clicks, 1 menu memorized.

good.label: AGENTIC UI
good.html:
"Get me last month's sales as a CSV."

The UI animates through the same 5 steps so you can see what it did. Click "stop" or "undo" if you want.

annotations.text: ↖ the UI narrates what it's doing, step by step

blocks.html:
You: Find the customers we lost last quarter and draft a win-back email.
↳ searching churn report…
↳ found 47 accounts
↳ drafting email template…

blocks.title: Under the hood — what makes a UI 'agentic' (and not annoying)
blocks.html:
Three things matter. Transparency: the user sees what the system is doing, not just the result. Steerability: they can interrupt, redirect, or undo at any point. Locality: actions happen in the place the user is already looking, not in some separate "AI tab."

Get those wrong and you've built a magic box that occasionally drops a turd on the user's lap. Get them right and the app starts to feel like it's collaborating with you.

items.question: A 'Submit' button vs. an AI 'do this' input — which is agentic?
items.answer: Trick question — neither, automatically. What makes a UI agentic isn't the input style, it's whether the system chooses and executes actions on the user's behalf, with visibility.

items.question: What's the biggest failure mode of agentic UI?
items.answer: The system silently doing something the user didn't intend — and the user not noticing until it's too late. The cure is loud, visible, reversible steps.

blocks.prompt: Take an app you use daily. What's one task that would be much better as an agentic flow — and what should still stay a manual button?

outro.title: That's the field guide.
outro.subtitle: Now go build something weird with it.

stickies.html:
✎ next volume idea:
"how to actually build one of these things, with code"

✎ remember:
the goal isn't to memorize. the goal is to recognize these patterns when you see them in the wild.
